title: DSLs as Harnesses
description: Part 3 of a 6-part series on AI-native computing. A deliberately limited language is what makes AI-generated software trustworthy for someone who cannot read code. Companion video to the essay of the same name.

beats.text: D S Ls as harnesses. This is part three of six. Part one claimed that computing's future rests on two pillars: the computer that understands, and the computer that builds. But a computer that builds leaves a question hanging. Who checks its work? This part is the answer.

eyebrow.label: Part 3 of 6 · Beyond the Sea of App Icons

slide.title: DSLs as **Harnesses**
slide.subtitle: The computer that {accent:builds} — and the language that makes its work {primary:safe to trust}
slide.author: Satoshi Nakajima

beats.text: Start with a story. The team behind SWE-agent, a coding agent, studied what made it succeed. The most valuable component was not the model. It was a linter. A simple checker that ran on every edit — and rejected broken changes the moment they were made, before one mistake could snowball into ten. The model supplied the intelligence. A guardrail supplied the reliability.
beats.imagePrompt: Editorial illustration on a deep navy blue background with glowing cyan and violet accents. A small luminous humanoid figure of light works intently at a floating desk, writing code that streams upward as glowing glyphs, all inside a large elegant structured frame — a geometric scaffold of thin bright cyan lines enclosing and supporting the workspace. At one edge, where a stray line of the figure's work touches the frame, the scaffold glows warmly, gently catching the mistake. Painterly, futuristic but warm, cinematic lighting, no text, no logos.

beats.text: That finding became the prevailing lesson of applied AI engineering. The harness — the designed environment an agent works inside — matters more than the model. The same model, in a better harness, does dramatically better work. And here is this essay's narrower claim: a deliberately limited language is one of the most powerful harnesses you can build.

items: The tools the agent can call
The format of what it sees

content.value: Same model, better harness — **dramatically better work.**

content.text: A deliberately limited language is a harness.

beats.text: Why would less power help? A general-purpose language can express anything. Which means there are infinite ways to go wrong — and nothing in the language says whether a program is right for your problem. Given that much freedom, agents thrash. A small language flips it. Fewer things, said in fewer ways, with a checker that says yes or no before anything runs. You haven't handed the agent a tool. You've reshaped the space it has to search.

slide.title: General-purpose code vs. a small language

right.title: A DSL

items.text: Yes or no — before anything runs

right.footer: Not a tool — a reshaped search space

beats.text: That is the whole trade, in one line. A DSL is a powerful harness precisely because it gives up power.

slide.quote: A DSL is a powerful harness precisely because it {primary:gives up power.}
slide.author: DSLs as Harnesses

beats.text: Make that trade, and four good things fall out for free. The schema is the record — intent lives in a document you can read. The validator is free feedback — mistakes are caught the instant they're made. The grammar forces correctness — there's no room to invent options that don't exist. And the unit of the language is the unit of context — the agent fixes one piece without rethinking the whole.

items.title: 1 · The schema is the record
items.description: Intent lives in a document you can read and diff.

items.title: 2 · The validator is free feedback
items.description: Mistakes caught the instant they're made.

items.title: 3 · The grammar forces correctness
items.description: No room to invent options that don't exist.

items.title: 4 · The unit of language is the unit of context
items.description: Fix one piece without holding the whole.

beats.text: Here's the part I enjoy. This very video is the argument. It was written by an agent, in MulmoScript — a small JSON language for describing presentations. A validator checked every beat before a single frame was rendered. The agent never touched a pixel; a renderer that never guesses drew what you're watching. The medium is the argument.

items.title: An agent writes MulmoScript
items.description: A JSON document of beats — never pixels

items.title: A renderer that never guesses
items.description: Same script, same video — diff, edit, re-render

beats.text: Now hand that power to someone who has never seen code. She says: track my rent, and make it recur monthly. Give me a year-at-a-glance view. A working app appears — live. She never sees a schema. She never sees a line of HTML. There's a name for this: vibe crafting.
beats.imagePrompt: Editorial illustration on a deep navy blue background with glowing cyan and violet accents. A woman in a cozy home office speaks casually toward the air, and in front of her a small elegant application is assembling itself out of light — a tidy table of records, a small monthly calendar, a rising chart — all held inside a soft glowing geometric frame. Thin streams of light flow from her words into the forming app. Painterly, warm and human, cinematic lighting, no text, no logos.

beats.text: But possible is not the same as safe. A developer who vibe-codes still reads the diff, runs the tests, owns the failure. An end user has none of that — and shouldn't need it. You can't ship ninety percent correct. So the harness supplies the safety she can't. The language can only say things that are safe to render. And anything less than valid never loads.

right.title: The end user's safety net: the harness

items.text: The DSL can only say what's safe to render

beats.text: One hazard. The same rigidity that makes a small language safe can turn it into a cage. When the language can't say what the user needs, the agent gives up — or contorts it into something it was never meant to hold. Well-designed DSLs all answer the same way: a deliberate exit to a more expressive layer, exactly where the language runs out.

slide.title: Design the **escape hatch**

items.title: Record → seeded chat
items.description: A record that needs human judgment opens a chat with a full agent

items.title: Schema → custom view
items.description: Restaurants as a map, a portfolio as a chart — sandboxed HTML, not contorted fields

items.title: Script → external media
items.description: MulmoScript embeds arbitrary assets instead of faking them in its primitives

slide.footer: A harness with no exit forces the agent to fail — or lie.

beats.text: So here is the honest cost: this is a dial, and both ends are failure. Pin down too much, and the language becomes a cage. Pin down too little, and you're back to flaky chat — nothing checked, nothing guaranteed. The craft is not maximizing constraint. The craft is choosing where the dial sits.

slide.title: Both ends of the dial are failure

items: Can't say what the user needs
The agent contorts it — or gives up

callout.text: The craft is {primary:**where the dial sits.**}
callout.label: The craft

beats.text: You might think guardrails are a crutch for weak models — that smarter models won't need them. I think the opposite. The smarter the model, the more we delegate, and the more the value shifts to verifying its work. Andrej Karpathy put it well: less Iron Man robots, more Iron Man suits. A DSL is what the suit is made of. A small document you can read at a glance. A validator that checks what a glance can't. A renderer that guarantees what you verified is what will run.

eyebrow.label: Why this matters more as models improve

content.value: A **small document** a human verifies at a glance.
The **validator** catches what a glance can't.
The **deterministic renderer** runs exactly what you approved.

callout.text: "Less Iron Man robots — {primary:more Iron Man suits.}" — Andrej Karpathy, 2025
callout.label: The suit

beats.text: This was part three of six — and the short version. The full essay has the schemas, the examples, and the counterarguments. Next, part four follows the logic to its end. If a schema can be the harness, then the application itself becomes data — and the model becomes the runtime.

slide.title: DSLs as **Harnesses**
slide.subtitle: Part 4: {primary:Applications Become Data} — the schema is the app, the model is the runtime
chips: 5 · Software for an audience of one
